description: Table 2A dialog: No-draw Five Card Draw, Vivian (Flamingo), Phase 2. Classical probability P = f/t, hot hand fallacy. Variable bet sizes 5/10/20. Standard rotation. Gate: procedural fraction, conceptual hot-hand rebuttal, transfer.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Main Room. Different table, different rules.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Vivian got this idea — no trading cards, just play what you're dealt. Kept bugging management until they gave her a table. So here we are.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One more thing. In the Main Room we rotate — who bets first switches every hand. Vivian opens the first one.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: I've been on a run for three months. Seventeen hands in a row. The cards are with me right now — I can feel it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Watch how she bets when she's winning. You'll see the pattern.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One more thing — bigger room, bigger stakes. Bets here are 5, 10, or 20 seeds. Your call on how much to put in.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Watch her bet size.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Keep tracking.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Notice anything about when she goes big?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good hand.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Vivian's going to remember that.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Vivian had it. Keep going.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Mark it. Keep tracking.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Good fold.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Folded. Sometimes that's right.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: See? I told you. When I'm hot, I'm hot. The cards know.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: She's been betting bigger after each win. Look at your frequency table. Does the data support what she's saying?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You've played enough hands here. Let's look at what your table actually shows.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.textTemplate: Look at your frequency table. You've seen {{highCardCount}} High Card hands out of {{totalHands}} total. What's the relative frequency as a percentage?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: That's relative frequency — what you actually observed. Count divided by total. Your frequency table does this math automatically; now you know what it's computing.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: A bit high. Check your table — High Card count divided by total hands, then multiply by 100.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Lower than expected? Make sure you're using your total hands played, not just a few.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Relative frequency = count / total, expressed as a percentage. Look at your table: divide your High Card count by total hands and multiply by 100.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Now — Vivian. She bets bigger when she's been winning. She thinks the cards are with her. Your frequency table says otherwise.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Vivian says 'I've won three in a row — the cards are with me.' What does your hand type distribution tell you about why that reasoning fails? Check all that apply.

options.text: The hand type frequencies are consistent — Three of a Kind doesn't come up more often after a few wins.
Since Vivian has been winning, she's more likely to get strong cards next hand.
Each hand is dealt fresh. The deck doesn't know or care what happened last hand.
The hand type distribution is the same regardless of recent win/loss history.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Right. Hand type frequencies are steady — no hot streaks. Each deal is independent. Vivian's wrong, and your table proves it.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One of those is Vivian's own belief. Which ones does your frequency table actually back up?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. What does the data actually show about whether hand quality changes after wins?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Hand type frequencies don't shift after winning streaks. Each deal is independent. The deck has no memory of whether Vivian won the last three hands.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: One more. Same idea, different context.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: A coin has landed heads five times in a row. A player says 'I'm on a hot streak — it's going to be heads again.' Which of these are true? Check all that apply.

options.text: The probability of heads on the next flip is still 1/2.
Five heads in a row means heads is 'running hot' and more likely to continue.
Each coin flip is independent — past results don't change future probabilities.
The coin is 'due' for a tails because it's been heads too many times.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The hot hand fallacy and the gambler's fallacy are mirror images — one says streaks continue, the other says they correct. Both are wrong for the same reason: independence.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Two of those sound like Vivian — and Lucky. Which ones does probability actually support?

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Almost. {{needToCheck}} more to check, {{needToUncheck}} to uncheck. A fair coin doesn't know its own history.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: Each flip is independent. P(heads) = 1/2 every single time, no matter how many heads came before. Streaks are real — they're just not predictive.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: You understand independent events. And you know why both Vivian and Lucky are wrong — they're making the same mistake from opposite directions.

nodes.speaker: chief-dodo
nodes.text: The next table gets into how we count all the ways a hand can happen. That's where the fractions come from.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: Five.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: Ten.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: Twenty. I feel good about this one.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: I'm in.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: Check.

nodes.speaker: vivian
nodes.text: Not this one.
